bibliographic.citation: Geoffrey Chaucer, Troilus & Criseyde: A New Edition of ‘The Book of Troilus,’ ed. Barry A. Windeatt (London and New York: Longman, 1984).
bibliographic.title: Troilus & Criseyde: A New Edition of ‘The Book of Troilus’

entities.label: Windeatt 1984 edition of Troilus and Criseyde
notes: The printed edition includes facing Italian Il Filostrato, glosses, notes, variants, and bibliography; these are not all present in the electronic text.
The Cambridge Faculty of English describes it as presenting textual variants of the extant Troilus manuscripts.

rights.basis: Chaucer died in 1400 and the underlying work predates modern copyright by centuries.
The edition was published in 1984 and its living editor was born in 1950; no public-domain dedication or open license for the edition was verified.
rights.component: Windeatt's 1984 selection, edited text, apparatus, glosses, notes, and other original editorial contribution
notes: This does not place Chaucer's underlying text under copyright.

entities.description: An aggregate description of the multiple surviving manuscript witnesses collated in modern critical editions; it is deliberately not a manuscript object or a claim of one base witness.
entities.label: Extant manuscript tradition of Troilus and Criseyde
notes: Consult Windeatt's list of manuscripts and apparatus for sigla and reading-level dependencies.

The public thumbnail viewer exposed only fol. 1r during direct inspection; no complete public 120-leaf facsimile or bulk download was verified.
The ignored local cache is a research copy and does not independently grant redistribution rights.

next_steps: Ask U-M and the Oxford Text Archive for the accession and permission record for U-1689-A.
Do not infer rights in Windeatt's apparatus from Chaucer's public-domain text or U-M's item label.
openQuestions.question: What permission or rights determination allowed OTA and U-M to distribute a text keyed from Windeatt 1984, and does it permit commercial redistribution of this XML-derived text?

next_steps: Consult Windeatt's full introduction, list of manuscripts, and apparatus; record individual witness edges only where explicitly supported.
openQuestions.question: Which witnesses and editorial principles determine each reading in Windeatt's base text?

reviewNotes: The aggregate manuscript-tradition entity prevents a false single-witness lineage claim.
U-M's current No Copyright–United States statement, the older OTA personal-scholarly-use notice in the XML, and possible copyright in Windeatt's 1984 editorial contribution are recorded separately.
Morgan MS M.817 is included as an accessible representative witness, not as the sole or stated base manuscript of Windeatt's edition.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy or version of University of Michigan CME item Troilus, distributed through Oxford Text Archive identifier U-1689-A and keyed from Barry A. Windeatt's 1984 critical edition. Windeatt's edition is eclectic and reports variants from the extant manuscript tradition; it is not a transcription of one manuscript. The electronic text omits the printed edition's front and back matter, editorial notes, bibliography, and parallel Italian Il Filostrato.

catalogSummary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of composition not securely established; narrative set in Troy
catalogSummary.title: Troilus and Criseyde

catalogingSubject.label: Troilus and Criseyde
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's five-book Middle English poem, as represented by the text selected from Windeatt's 1984 edition in CME/OTA item Troilus. The cataloging subject excludes Windeatt's parallel Il Filostrato, apparatus, and bibliography, which the XML says were omitted.

contentParts.label: Book I
contentParts.summary: Troilus first sees and falls in love with Criseyde; Pandarus undertakes to mediate.

contentParts.label: Book II
contentParts.summary: Pandarus advances the courtship and brings Troilus and Criseyde into communication.

contentParts.label: Book III
contentParts.summary: The lovers are united and experience the poem's central period of joy.

contentParts.label: Book IV
contentParts.summary: Criseyde is exchanged for Antenor, separating the lovers and provoking debate over destiny and free will.

contentParts.label: Book V
contentParts.summary: Criseyde remains with the Greeks and Diomede; Troilus dies in battle and looks back on earthly love from the heavens.

notes: The date is approximate: the repository XML supplies 1385, and this record does not infer unsupported multi-year bounds or normalize the approximation to an exact year.
This is a date for an extant copy, not composition.

extent.description: Five books; repository XML contains 1,178 line groups and 8,252 L elements, including embedded songs and letters as well as rhyme-royal stanzas.

formStatements.description: A five-book narrative poem predominantly in seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas, with embedded songs and letters.

notes: No single dialect region is assigned: an eclectic edition of a multi-witness literary work cannot safely inherit one witness's scribal localization.
The line and stanza counts are encoding metrics; embedded songs and letters complicate simple rhyme-royal stanza arithmetic.

next_steps: Consult the dating discussion in Windeatt's introduction and current Chaucer scholarship before adopting narrower phases.
openQuestions.question: Can the composition and revision chronology be narrowed beyond the conventional mid-1380s range without overstating disputed internal and historical evidence?

next_steps: Consult the full textual introduction and apparatus; do not assign one manuscript's dialect to the work as a whole.
openQuestions.question: Which manuscript dialect and normalization choices underlie each form in Windeatt's eclectic text?

notes: This does not identify a precise place of composition or a manuscript dialect.

scope.description: Creative retelling drawing especially on Boccaccio's Il Filostrato and on Boethian materials
Extant-manuscript variants in the immediate edition

summaries.value: A five-book Middle English narrative poem recounting Troilus's love for Criseyde during the Trojan War, their union through Pandarus, Criseyde's exchange to the Greek camp and relationship with Diomede, and Troilus's death and final vision beyond earthly mutability. Chaucer transforms Boccaccio's Il Filostrato through sustained reflection on love, agency, Fortune, providence, and authorship.
This record describes Chaucer's poem. The 1984 printed source also contains Italian parallel text and modern editorial matter that the electronic edition expressly omits.

notes: Used in the subtitle of Windeatt's edition and reflected in Chaucer's closing address to his book.

Chaucer transforms Boccaccio's Il Filostrato through sustained reflection on love, agency, Fortune, providence, and authorship.
